critic_raw: The design uses 'ejection fraction' as a feature with timestamps. Ejection fraction is measured via echocardiogram, which is typically done once per admission (not repeatedly). Including it as a time-stamped sequence feature implies it updates over time, but for most admissions it would have a single measurement. This creates a sparse feature in the sequence model that appears only once — the sequence model would need to learn to attend to this single measurement among many other time-stamped events. Does this differ from how the logistic regression baseline uses ejection fraction?
defender_raw: Round 1: The design includes ejection fraction (with result timestamps) as a laboratory value in the sequence model. For a single-measurement feature like ejection fraction, the sequence model would see exactly one event in the sequence at the time of the echocardiogram. The logistic regression baseline uses 'min/max/mean/last value' for each vital and lab — for a single measurement, these aggregates are all the same value. Both models see the same ejection fraction information, just represented differently (a single timestamped event vs. a single aggregate value). The sequence model may learn to use the timing of the ejection fraction measurement (when in the admission it was obtained) as additional context — which is a legitimate architectural advantage.

rounds.critic_followup: If ejection fraction is measured early in admission for all patients, its timing is not informative — all patients have similar measurement timing. The sequence model's attention to timing would not add value for this feature. Is there a risk that the sequence model wastes capacity attending to timing of uniform-timing features?
rounds.defender_followup: Attention mechanisms learn to weight features by their predictive value — if ejection fraction timing is uniform, the model would learn low attention weights for its timing. The feature value itself (reduced ejection fraction) is highly predictive for heart failure readmission, and the sequence model can attend to it regardless of when it was measured. If all patients have the same measurement timing, the timing signal contributes zero entropy; the model would correctly ignore it. This is not a capacity waste — attention weights are learned, not hardcoded.

adjudication_raw: One concern: single-measurement features (ejection fraction) may not benefit from sequence modeling timing. Resolved: both models see same feature; attention learns to ignore uninformative timing signals; ejection fraction value itself is predictive. Verdict: defense_wins.
